title: Evaluating AI Use Cases
description: Learn a practical framework for identifying, evaluating, and scoping AI use cases — whether you're a product manager building a roadmap, a designer prototyping experiences, or a business leader evaluating AI investments.
learningObjectives: Apply a structured framework to evaluate whether AI is the right solution for a problem
Distinguish between high-value and low-value AI use cases
Define success metrics and scope for an AI-powered feature

sections.title: Finding the Right Problems for AI
sections.content:
Not every problem benefits from AI. The most common mistake organizations make is starting with the technology ("let's use AI!") rather than the problem ("this process is slow and error-prone — could AI help?"). Choosing the right problem is the single most important decision in any AI initiative, and it deserves careful thought before anyone writes a line of code.

AI excels at tasks that revolve around language and structured reasoning. **Language understanding** is one of its core strengths — interpreting unstructured text, answering questions about documents, and extracting meaning from messy inputs that would take humans considerable time to process. Closely related is **generation with guardrails**, where AI drafts content, writes code, or produces variations within defined constraints. These generative tasks benefit enormously from AI's ability to produce fluent, context-aware output at scale.

Beyond generation, AI is particularly effective at **classification and routing** — sorting items into categories, prioritizing queues, and directing requests to the right team or workflow. It also handles **transformation** tasks well, such as converting between formats, summarizing lengthy documents, translating across languages, and restructuring data into new shapes. Finally, AI brings real capability to **analysis and reasoning**, where it can compare options, identify patterns across text, and evaluate information against a set of criteria.

On the other hand, certain categories of tasks remain a poor fit. When a task demands **deterministic correctness** — where even a 1% error rate is unacceptable, as in financial calculations or compliance checks — AI alone will not be sufficient. Tasks requiring **real-time data** also present challenges, because AI does not have access to live information unless it is connected to external tools. Similarly, AI lacks **institutional memory**; it cannot recall past conversations or organizational history without a retrieval system feeding it that context. And of course, **physical world interaction** falls outside AI's reach entirely — it processes text and images, not physical objects.

The sweet spot for AI is tasks that humans can do but that are time-consuming, repetitive, or don't scale — and where the occasional imperfect output is acceptable or can be caught by review.

playground.description: Test whether a task is a good fit for AI. Describe a real workplace task and see how Claude handles it — this is exactly how you gauge feasibility before committing to a project.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a helpful assistant. The user will describe a workplace task. Attempt to perform the task based on whatever information they provide. After completing the task, add a brief section labeled FEASIBILITY NOTE where you reflect honestly on how well-suited this type of task is for AI: what went well, what was difficult, and what limitations you encountered.
playground.userMessage:
Here is a task from our operations team: We receive customer feedback emails and need to categorize each one as billing, technical, feature-request, praise, or other — and flag any that sound urgent. Here are three sample emails:

1. "I was charged twice for my subscription this month. Please fix this ASAP, I need the money back."
2. "Love the new dashboard! The charts are exactly what I needed."
3. "Would be great if you could add a dark mode option. Not urgent, just a nice-to-have."

Categorize each email and flag any urgent ones.

sections.title: The AI Use Case Evaluation Framework
sections.content:
When evaluating a potential AI use case, assess it across five dimensions. Rather than assigning numerical scores that imply false precision, the goal is to identify red flags — dimensions where serious concerns could derail the project. A use case with no red flags and several strong dimensions is worth pursuing. A single red flag does not necessarily kill a use case, but it does mean you need a mitigation plan before investing.

**1. Value — Is this worth doing?**
The central question is whether AI meaningfully changes the outcome for real people. Ask: How many people are affected, and how often? What do they do today without AI, and what is painful about it? Is the value primarily time savings, quality improvement, or enabling something previously impossible? A red flag here is when the value case relies on theoretical benefits ("AI could...") rather than observable pain ("our team spends 20 hours a week on..."). Another red flag: the use case solves a problem that a simpler tool — a spreadsheet formula, a search feature, a template — could handle just as well.

**2. Feasibility — Can you validate this with Claude right now?**
Do not speculate about feasibility — test it. Open Claude and try the core task with a well-crafted prompt using representative inputs. Ask: Does Claude handle the typical case well? What happens with edge cases and ambiguous inputs? Does quality hold up across different examples, or is it inconsistent? A red flag is when the task requires capabilities that fall outside language understanding and generation, such as precise numerical computation, real-time data access, or pattern detection across large structured datasets. Another red flag: if your test prompt requires extensive hand-holding to get reasonable output, production use will be fragile.

**3. Risk — What is the blast radius of a mistake?**
Every AI system produces errors. The question is what happens when it does. Ask: If the AI gives a wrong output, who is affected and how severely? Is there a natural review step where a human catches errors before they matter? Can errors be reversed, or are they permanent? A red flag is when errors are high-consequence and there is no feasible human review step — for example, an AI that autonomously sends medical guidance to patients. A human-in-the-loop pattern (where AI drafts and a human approves) is the most reliable way to reduce this risk.

**4. Data readiness — Does the context exist?**
Claude needs context to perform well, and that context has to come from somewhere. Ask: Do you have the documents, examples, or structured data that Claude would need? Is that data clean, accessible via API or file, and representative of the full range of real inputs? Are there privacy or compliance constraints on using this data with an external AI service? A red flag is when the necessary data is scattered across systems with no easy way to assemble it, or when the data you have is not representative of what the system will actually encounter in production.

**5. User acceptance — Will people trust and use it?**
The best AI feature fails if the people it is built for do not adopt it. Ask: Are the target users comfortable with AI in their workflow, or will there be resistance? Does the AI interaction fit naturally into how they already work, or does it require them to change their process? Is there a graceful fallback when the AI is not helpful? A red flag is when the target users have expressed skepticism about AI or when the interaction model requires users to abandon a workflow they are comfortable with. Early involvement of actual users — even just showing them a prototype and asking for reactions — dramatically reduces this risk.

**Reading the results:** Look at the pattern across all five dimensions. A strong use case has no red flags and clear strength in at least value and feasibility. If you find one red flag, develop a specific mitigation plan before proceeding. Two or more red flags suggest you should either fundamentally rethink the approach or choose a different use case. The goal is not a score — it is a clear-eyed understanding of where the risks are and whether you can address them.

playground.description: Run a red-flag assessment on a real use case. Describe a potential AI use case and Claude will evaluate it across all five dimensions, identifying red flags and strengths.
playground.systemPrompt: You are an AI strategy consultant helping evaluate potential AI use cases. When the user describes a use case, assess it across these five dimensions: (1) Value — is this worth doing? (2) Feasibility — can AI actually do this well? (3) Risk — what is the blast radius of a mistake? (4) Data readiness — does the necessary context exist? (5) User acceptance — will people trust and use it? For each dimension, answer the diagnostic questions and clearly state whether you see a RED FLAG or a STRENGTH. At the end, give an overall recommendation: proceed, proceed with mitigation plan, or reconsider.
playground.userMessage: We're a 50-person law firm. Our paralegals spend about 15 hours per week reading through discovery documents (depositions, contracts, correspondence) and writing summaries for the attorneys. We want to use AI to automatically generate these summaries so paralegals can focus on higher-value work. The documents are stored in our case management system and contain sensitive client information. Our paralegals are generally skeptical of AI tools.

sections.title: Scoping and Measuring Success
sections.content:
Once you've identified a promising use case, scope it tightly before building. The most successful AI features start narrow and expand — not because teams lack ambition, but because narrow scope makes it possible to learn quickly, demonstrate value, and build the organizational confidence needed for larger investments.

**Start with one workflow, not a platform.** Instead of "AI-powered customer support," start with "AI categorizes incoming support tickets by urgency and topic." Instead of "AI content generation," start with "AI drafts first versions of weekly team status updates." This tight scoping forces clarity about what exactly the AI is doing, who is using it, and how you will know if it is working.

**Define measurable success criteria before you build.** Without concrete metrics, AI projects tend to drift into vague assessments like "it seems helpful." Strong success criteria cover several angles. **Accuracy** establishes what percentage of outputs need to be correct and how you will measure correctness. **Speed** captures how much faster the AI-assisted workflow is compared to the manual one. **Adoption** sets a target for what percentage of users should be actively using the feature within a defined timeframe, such as 30 days. And **satisfaction** measures how users rate the quality of AI outputs, whether through a simple thumbs up/down mechanism or a short rating scale.

**Plan for the feedback loop.** The best AI features improve over time because they collect signal about what works and what doesn't. Design feedback mechanisms from the start so you can build on real usage data:
- Let users rate or correct AI outputs
- Log the inputs that produce poor results
- Track which categories of requests have the lowest accuracy
- Use this data to improve prompts, add examples, or refine guardrails

**Set a review cadence.** AI performance can drift as usage patterns change, new types of inputs appear, or the underlying task evolves. Plan monthly reviews of accuracy metrics, user feedback, and edge cases. This is how you catch degradation before users notice — and how you identify opportunities to expand the feature's scope based on demonstrated success.

sections.title: Evaluate a Use Case
sections.content: Apply the framework to a real scenario.

exercise.prompt:
A mid-size e-commerce company (200 employees, 50,000 orders/month) is considering three AI use cases:

1. **AI Product Descriptions** — Automatically generate product descriptions from product specs and images for their catalog of 5,000 products.

2. **AI Fraud Detection** — Use Claude to analyze order patterns and flag potentially fraudulent transactions before they're processed.

3. **AI Customer Email Drafts** — When a support agent opens a customer email, AI generates a draft response that the agent can edit and send.

For each use case, run it through the five-dimension red-flag assessment (Value, Feasibility, Risk, Data Readiness, User Acceptance). For each dimension, answer the diagnostic questions from the framework and identify any red flags.

Then, for the use case you consider most promising, **use the Claude companion to actually test feasibility**: give it a representative task (e.g., draft a product description from a spec, or draft a customer reply to a complaint) and evaluate the output quality. Does your feasibility assessment hold up once you see real output?

Based on your full evaluation — framework analysis plus hands-on testing — which use case would you recommend they start with, and why?
hints: For each dimension, ask the specific diagnostic questions: Who is affected and how often? Can you test it with Claude right now? What is the blast radius of a mistake?
Fraud detection is the trickiest one — think about whether the core task (detecting patterns in numerical transaction data) matches what language models are actually good at
Actually try one task in the Claude companion. Testing feasibility with real input is more valuable than guessing at it
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should evaluate all three use cases using the red-flag framework, identifying specific flags rather than assigning numerical scores. Strong responses will: (1) Find no red flags for AI Product Descriptions on feasibility and value (bulk content generation is an AI sweet spot) but flag data readiness if specs are inconsistent; (2) Identify clear red flags for AI Fraud Detection on feasibility (Claude is not designed for pattern detection across numerical transaction data — this is better suited to traditional ML) and risk (missed fraud is high-consequence with no natural review step); (3) Find AI Customer Email Drafts strong across dimensions, noting the human-in-the-loop pattern (agent reviews before sending) as a natural risk mitigator. The recommendation should be either Product Descriptions or Email Drafts with a clear explanation of why Fraud Detection is a poor fit for LLMs. Bonus for actually testing a use case in Claude and reflecting on whether the output matched their feasibility assessment.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content: Reflect on how to apply the evaluation framework.

exercise.prompt: Your CEO just came back from a conference excited about AI and wants to "add AI to everything." They've asked you to identify the top AI opportunity across the company. Describe your approach: what questions would you ask, how would you prioritize opportunities, and how would you present your recommendation to get buy-in from both the CEO and the teams who would implement it?
exercise.evaluationCriteria: Should describe a structured approach: (1) Start by auditing current workflows for high-volume, repetitive language tasks — not jumping to solutions; (2) Interview teams to find pain points and time sinks; (3) Apply the red-flag evaluation framework to top candidates, identifying specific flags rather than assigning scores; (4) Test feasibility of the top candidates by actually trying the core task with Claude; (5) Recommend starting with one use case that has no red flags and clear value, rather than pursuing many at once; (6) Present with concrete metrics (time saved, error reduction) and a mitigation plan for any yellow-flag dimensions rather than vague AI hype; (7) Propose a tightly scoped pilot with clear success criteria and timeline. Bonus for mentioning the importance of choosing a use case with a visible win to build organizational confidence in AI.

sections.title: Key Takeaway
sections.content: The best AI use cases combine high value with high feasibility and manageable risk. Start by finding problems where language understanding or generation is the bottleneck, scope tightly to a single workflow, define measurable success criteria before building, and plan for continuous improvement through user feedback. Remember that a well-chosen narrow use case that delivers clear results will do more for your organization than an ambitious initiative that never quite proves its value.
